questions.question: Explain the Waterfall model and list its limitations.
keyPoints: Waterfall is a sequential linear model with phases: Requirements, Design, Implementation, Verification, Maintenance
Limitations: Inflexible to changes, late testing, customer cannot see product until end, high risk if requirements change

questions.question: Describe the key features of Agile methodology.
keyPoints: Iterative and incremental development
Emphasis on customer collaboration and feedback
Welcome changing requirements even late in development
Working software is primary measure of progress
Self-organizing cross-functional teams
Regular adaptation to changing circumstances

questions.question: What is the difference between Verification and Validation? Provide examples.
keyPoints: Verification: Checking if software meets specified requirements - 'Are we building it right?' (e.g., review, inspection, static testing)
Validation: Checking if software meets user needs - 'Are we building the right product?' (e.g., user acceptance testing, beta testing)
Both are essential but occur at different stages

questions.question: Explain the Scrum framework with its roles and ceremonies.
keyPoints: Scrum is an Agile framework for managing complex projects
Roles: Product Owner (defines features), Scrum Master (facilitates), Development Team (delivers)
Ceremonies: Sprint Planning, Daily Stand-up, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective
Sprints are time-boxed iterations (2-4 weeks)

questions.question: What is the V-Model? How does it relate testing to development?
keyPoints: V-Model is an extension of Waterfall with development and testing in parallel
Left side: Development phases (Requirements → Architecture → Detailed Design → Coding)
Right side: Testing phases (Unit → Integration → System → Acceptance)
Each development phase has corresponding testing phase

questions.question: Explain DevOps and its benefits in software development.
keyPoints: DevOps combines development and operations practices
Aims to shorten development lifecycle and provide continuous delivery
Practices include: CI/CD, automation, monitoring, infrastructure as code
Benefits: Faster delivery, improved quality, better collaboration, reduced failures

questions.question: Describe the Spiral Model and its risk management approach.
keyPoints: Spiral model combines iterative development with systematic risk analysis
Four phases in each cycle: Planning, Risk Analysis, Engineering, Evaluation
Continues in spirals until project is complete
Each iteration reduces project risk through analysis
Suitable for large, complex projects with unclear requirements

questions.question: Compare Waterfall, Agile, and Spiral models. Under what circumstances would you choose each?
keyPoints: Waterfall: Sequential, rigid, good for well-defined stable requirements, small projects
Agile: Flexible, iterative, customer collaboration, best for projects with changing requirements
Spiral: Risk-driven, combines benefits of both, good for large complex projects
Selection criteria: Requirements stability, project size, team experience, customer involvement, risk factors
Modern projects often use hybrid approaches combining elements

questions.question: Explain the complete SDLC process from requirements gathering to maintenance. What are the deliverables at each phase?
keyPoints: Requirements: SRS document, use cases, user stories
Design: Architecture documents, design specifications, database design
Implementation: Source code, unit tests, integration components
Testing: Test plans, test cases, defect reports
Deployment: Deployed system, user manuals, training materials
Maintenance: Bug fixes, updates, performance improvements
Each phase has specific inputs and outputs

questions.question: Describe the Agile Scrum methodology in detail. How does it ensure continuous improvement and stakeholder satisfaction?
keyPoints: Scrum is an Agile framework with time-boxed sprints
Roles: Product Owner manages backlog, Scrum Master facilitates, Development Team delivers
Artifacts: Product Backlog, Sprint Backlog, Increment
Events: Sprint Planning (2-4 hours), Daily Stand-up (15 min), Sprint Review (2-4 hours), Sprint Retrospective (1-2 hours)
Continuous improvement through retrospective - team reflects and adapts
Stakeholder satisfaction through regular demos, working increments, adaptability to change

questions.question: What is DevOps? Explain the practices of Continuous Integration, Continuous Delivery, and Continuous Deployment. How do they transform software delivery?
keyPoints: DevOps bridges development and operations teams
CI: Developers frequently merge code with automated build and test (multiple times daily)
CD: Code changes automatically prepared for release to production after passing tests
CD (Continuous Deployment): Automatically deploys all changes to production
Benefits: Faster delivery, smaller batches, early detection of issues, improved quality, reliable releases, better collaboration
